points.text: Profile the endpoint to log start/end times of each sequential call and pinpoint the longest‑running step.
Convert independent calls to run concurrently using async/await (Python asyncio) or Promise.all (JS) to reduce overall latency.
Rewrite the heavy SQL: select only needed columns, replace SELECT * and move complex joins into a materialized view or CTE.
Create composite indexes on all columns used in JOIN and WHERE clauses to eliminate full‑table scans.
Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the query, remove unnecessary joins, and add LIMIT/OFFSET where appropriate.
Set a client‑side timeout (e.g., 30 s) and a server‑side timeout (e.g., 60 s); return a clear error if exceeded.
Add a circuit‑breaker or retry policy that aborts the request after the timeout budget is hit, preventing cascading delays.
Cache the query result for a short TTL (e.g., 5 min) when data is read‑only to avoid repeated heavy joins.
Batch sequential calls into a single request or use async parallelism to reduce round‑trip latency.
Move complex joins to a pre‑aggregated materialized view refreshed off‑peak.
Select only required columns; replace SELECT * with explicit column list.
Add covering indexes on foreign‑key columns used in joins and filter predicates.
Rewrite joins using EXISTS or IN where appropriate to avoid unnecessary row multiplication.
Apply proper WHERE clauses early; push predicates to the smallest tables first.
Enable query plan caching; use prepared statements to avoid repeated parsing.
Set PostgreSQL statement_timeout (e.g., 30s) and client‑side timeout budget based on SLA.
Split sequential external calls into parallel async tasks to reduce overall latency.
Add a circuit‑breaker or retry with back‑off for each call to avoid cascading timeouts.
Profile the SQL with EXPLAIN; ensure indexes on all join and filter columns.
Replace SELECT * with only needed columns; use covering indexes to avoid lookups.
Consider denormalizing or materialized views for heavy multi‑table joins.
Add LIMIT/OFFSET or pagination to bound result size and execution time.
Cache frequent query results at the application layer to cut DB load.
Set per‑operation timeouts: e.g., 500 ms for HTTP calls, 1 s for DB query, total budget ≤2 s.
OUT_OF_SCOPE: The query pertains to software performance and SQL optimization, not within math_logic domain.
Split independent sequential calls into parallel tasks where thread‑safety permits, reducing overall latency.
Move complex joins to a stored procedure and ensure join columns have appropriate indexes.
Apply pagination or LIMIT clauses to restrict result set size and avoid excessive data processing.
Profile the query, add missing indexes, and consider denormalizing hot‑path tables to speed joins.
Configure DB driver timeout to be a safe fraction of the HTTP request timeout (e.g., 30 s of 60 s).
Implement a circuit‑breaker pattern to abort long‑running queries and return a graceful error.
Cache frequent query results or materialized views to reduce repeated heavy join execution.
